title: Wet-felted wrist warmers
subtitle: Short cuffed hand warmers worked over a plastic resist
excerpt: Make a pair of wrist warmers 10 cm long using merino roving laid over a plastic hand-shaped resist. Two cross-hatch layers shrink to a dense, stretchy cuff with a cut thumb opening. The resist must be cut 40-50% larger than the finished size to account for the shrinkage that gives wet-felted fabric its density.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from wet-felting resist techniques widely documented in UK and Scandinavian guild teaching materials. The two-layer cross-hatch method and 40-50% shrinkage allowance are standard published figures for fine merino roving at 19-21 micron. No single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
A waterproof template placed inside a wet-felted piece to create a hollow form. Removed after initial felting is complete.
The stage of wet felting where the fabric is agitated more vigorously, causing it to shrink and tighten into a firm, dense cloth.
The extra size built into a resist or layout to compensate for the reduction in dimensions during felting.

content.text: Wet-felted wrist warmers are worked over a flat plastic
resist
cut to the shape of the hand from the wrist to just below the knuckles, with no finger coverage. The
merino
roving
is laid in two cross-hatched layers over each side of the resist, wetted, rubbed to consolidate, then rolled and
fulled
until the fabric is firm and dense. The critical number to understand before cutting any resist is the
shrinkage allowance

: merino wet-felted in two layers typically shrinks by 40-50% in each dimension. If your finished wrist warmer needs to measure 10 cm from wrist to knuckle and 20 cm around, your resist must be at least 14-15 cm tall and 28-30 cm around. Skipping the calculation produces a piece that is too small to put on.
Cutting the resist

Trace around your hand on a sheet of bubble wrap or thick plastic, from just above the wrist crease to the base of the fingers. Close the outline to make a flat paddle shape with a slight thumb bulge on one side. Cut the shape out, then scale it up: add 40% to every dimension as your minimum shrinkage buffer. For an average adult hand measuring 9 cm wrist to knuckle and 18 cm circumference, the resist should be at least 12.5 cm tall with a circumference of 25 cm when folded. Cut two identical resists, one for each wrist warmer.
Method

Step 1: Lay the first side. Place your resist flat on the bamboo mat over a cotton tea towel. Pull thin wisps of merino roving and lay them horizontally across the resist, overlapping each wisp by about half its width and extending the fibres 2-3 cm beyond the resist edge. Cover the whole surface, then add a second layer of wisps laid vertically over the first. Two layers per side is the standard cross-hatch structure for a wrist-warmer weight fabric.

Step 2: Wet out the first side. Mist the fibres thoroughly with warm water using the spray bottle. Place a piece of bubble wrap over the surface, bubble side down, and press firmly with flat hands to wet the fibres all the way through. The roving should look translucent and lie flat. Add a few drops of washing-up liquid diluted in water to the spray bottle to help the fibres bond.

Step 3: Felt the first side lightly. Rub gently over the bubble wrap in circular motions for about 3-4 minutes. Lift the bubble wrap and check: the fibres should be starting to matt together and grip the resist. They should not be moving freely as loose roving any longer, but the fabric should still feel soft and have some give. Fold the overhanging fibres over the edge of the resist onto the back.

Step 4: Lay and felt the second side. Flip the whole piece over carefully so the resist is now on top of the felt. Lay two cross-hatch layers of roving on this second side, folding the resist edge fibres back to create neat borders. Wet out and rub as before. After 3-4 minutes the piece should feel coherent on both sides.

Step 5: Roll to full. Place the piece on the bamboo mat, roll up tightly, and roll it back and forth 50 times. Unroll, rotate the piece 90 degrees, and roll another 50 times. Repeat twice more. Check the fabric after each rolling sequence: as fulling progresses the piece will shrink noticeably and the texture will firm up. This stage usually takes 15-20 minutes of active rolling.

Step 6: Remove the resist. When the felt is firm enough that it holds its shape when you press the edges together, carefully slide out the resist through one open end. Continue fulling without the resist: fold the piece in half, squeeze and rub between your palms, and then roll for a further 20 repetitions on each axis. The fabric will tighten further once the resist is out.

Step 7: Cut the thumb opening. Once fully felted, mark the thumb position on the outside of the wrist warmer using a small pin: roughly 2-3 cm from the top edge on the outer thumb side, set about 1 cm in from the side seam. Cut a slit 3-4 cm long with the dressmaker's scissors. The felted fabric will not fray, so no finishing is needed around the opening. Try the warmer on and adjust if needed.

Step 8: Rinse and shape. Rinse both pieces thoroughly in warm water until no soap remains, then press out excess water in a folded tea towel. Shape the warmers over your hand while still damp to set the curve, then lay flat to dry away from direct heat.
Troubleshooting

items.problem: The two sides of the wrist warmer are not bonding together at the edges.
items.solution: The fibres folded over the resist edges were probably too short. Pull the piece apart gently, add fresh wisps of roving around the perimeter, rewet, and rub for several more minutes before rolling.

items.problem: The finished warmer is too small to get on.
items.solution: The resist was not cut large enough for the shrinkage rate of the wool used. For future pairs, measure the finished piece, calculate the actual shrinkage percentage, and scale the resist up accordingly. Finer micron merino shrinks more aggressively than coarser breeds.

items.problem: The surface has thin patches where the roving laid unevenly.
items.solution: Place the warmer flat and add a few wisps of roving over the thin area, wetting lightly and rubbing by hand until the patch integrates into the surrounding fabric. Then re-roll the piece for an additional 20 repetitions on each axis.

items.problem: The thumb slit is in the wrong position.
items.solution: Put the warmer on with the slit as cut and mark the correct thumb position on your skin with a washable pen. Transfer the mark to the fabric and cut a second small slit. A single small cut felts closed easily if you wet it and rub; the original misplaced slit can usually be needle-felted shut with a few dry passes.

content.text: Finishing and care

Wet-felted merino can be hand-washed in cool water with a small amount of wool wash. Do not wring or rub when wet; press water out gently in a towel and reshape flat to dry. The wrist warmers will continue to soften slightly with wear and washing.
